data: Long rectangular crystalline marble plaque. Ends squared, but clearly missing section on left of stone (perhaps due to a later reuse of the block), and roughness of right side perhaps indicates a broken right side. Rear uneven.

Two columns of Greek funerary texts. Right column intact, but left column is almost completely missing. Perhaps a missing epitaph on the right as well. 4 lines of Greek text in each column, text of both columns is roughly in line. The right column has an unusual stepped indentation of the left margin, with each line starting c.1 character further to the right than the last.
Letters are neat, evenly spaced, but finely incised on stone. Letters bear simple serifs. Epsilons have arrows in place of cross-bar. Short cross-bar of the theta in column 2, line 3.
Found in Termini Imerese, in the square of S. Antonino.
Antoniou, 2023-07-04. In the Depositi of Museo Civico Baldassare Romano, room 1, scaffold 16, shelf 9.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana

A large and well-displayed collection. It begins with three ante-rooms, in the first of which is a rich display of lithics (mainly Neolithic) from the surrounding area. A display of material from Himera (lion-head spouts from the Temple of Victory and red-figure craters being the principle pieces) and a numismatic gallery then follow before the main room which contains Hellenistic and Roman material from Termini Imerese itself. Small finds, sculpture and inscriptions predominate. The museum collection continues upstairs with non-archaeological material (and a single inscription). The courtyard of the building holds a large collection of architectural pieces and inscriptions
Via Marco Tullio Cicerone, 90018 - Termini Imerese (PA)
Iscrizione greche del museo civico di Termini Imerese
Antonietta Brugnone, «Iscrizione greche del museo civico di Termini Imerese», Kokalos 20 (1974): 218–64
Robert Crellin integrated lemmatizations into EpiDoc

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text after autopsy

English translation

Alex Antoniou

[Right column]: Herais, daughter of Herylos, farewell.

commentary

Given the presence of at least two epitaphs, it is likely that this plaque belonged to a family tomb. Herylos is a unique name, not attested elsewhere. Brugnone (1974) suggests that it could be a form of the documented Heryllos, or is a derivative of the Latin Herylus. Herais is an extremely common name in the empire, attested in Sicily only in the masculine on a lead sheet at Kamarina, ISic030053.
